description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures typically lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of all data or performing a business function outside the user's limits.
test_methods: Test IDOR by changing object IDs in requests
Verify regular users cannot access admin endpoints
Test horizontal privilege escalation (user A accessing user B's data)
Test vertical privilege escalation (user accessing admin functions)
Check for missing authorization on API endpoints
Test forced browsing to unauthorized URLs
fixes: Validate ownership on every resource access

description: Focuses on failures related to cryptography which often leads to sensitive data exposure or system compromise. Previously known as 'Sensitive Data Exposure'.
test_methods: Verify certificates are valid and not expired
Check for sensitive data in URLs, logs, cache
Test for sensitive data in transit (forms, APIs)
fixes: Use secure password hashing (bcrypt cost 12+, argon2id)

description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. The attacker's hostile data can trick the interpreter into executing unintended commands or accessing data without proper authorization.
test_methods: Test SQL injection in all input fields (forms, headers, cookies, JSON)
Test NoSQL injection in MongoDB queries
Test LDAP injection in directory queries
Test OS command injection in file operations
Test XSS in all reflected inputs
fixes: Escape output for context (HTML, JS, CSS, URL)
Use prepared statements for all DB queries

description: Insecure design is a broad category representing different weaknesses, expressed as 'missing or ineffective control design.' It's not about implementation bugs but about fundamental design flaws that cannot be fixed by perfect implementation.
test_methods: Review business logic for security flaws
Test rate limiting on sensitive operations
Test for race conditions in critical flows
Check for missing security requirements in design phase
fixes: Implement threat modeling in design phase
Use secure design patterns (zero trust, defense in depth)
Add security requirements to all features
Implement rate limiting on all sensitive endpoints
Design for security from the start

description: Security misconfiguration is the most commonly seen issue. This is commonly a result of insecure default configurations, incomplete or ad hoc configurations, open cloud storage, misconfigured HTTP headers, and verbose error messages containing sensitive information.
test_methods: Check for default credentials on all systems
Scan for open cloud storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS)
Test for verbose error messages/stack traces
Check HTTP security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
Check for unnecessary features enabled (directory listing, debug modes)
fixes: Use infrastructure as code with security baselines

description: You are likely vulnerable if you do not know the versions of all components you use (both client-side and server-side). This includes components you directly use as well as nested dependencies.
test_methods: Run dependency scanning (npm audit, pip-audit, Snyk, Dependabot)
Check for known CVEs in all dependencies
Monitor for new vulnerabilities in used components
Test for vulnerable components in CI/CD pipeline
fixes: Implement automated dependency scanning in CI/CD
Only use components from official sources
Patch within defined SLA based on severity

description: Confirmation of the user's identity, authentication, and session management is critical to protect against authentication-related attacks. There's also a need to confirm the identity of the user accessing the system.
test_methods: Check for account lockout/rate limiting on login
Test for weak password reset mechanisms
fixes: Use strong password policies (12+ chars, no common passwords)
Implement rate limiting on auth endpoints
Use secure session management (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite)

category: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Software and data integrity failures relate to code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations. Examples include software updates without integrity checks, CI/CD pipeline without proper access controls, and auto-update functionality without sufficient verification.
test_methods: Check for supply chain attack vectors
fixes: Sign all releases and verify signatures

category: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: This category is to help detect, escalate, and respond to active breaches. Without logging and monitoring, breaches cannot be detected. Insufficient logging, detection, monitoring, and active response occurs any time: auditable events are not logged, warnings/errors are not logged, logs are not monitored, etc.
test_methods: Check for logging of security events (login, failed login, access denied)
Verify logs contain sufficient context (user, IP, timestamp, action)
Check for alerting on anomalous activity
fixes: Set up alerts for anomalous patterns

description: SSRF flaws occur whenever a web application is fetching a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. It allows an attacker to coerce the application to send a crafted request to an unexpected destination, even when protected by a firewall, VPN, or another type of network ACL.
test_methods: Test all URL parameters for internal IP access
Test for gopher://, dict://, ftp:// protocols
fixes: Disable unnecessary URL schemes (file, gopher, dict)
Don't send raw responses to client
